meta.title: The Group Areas Act
meta.subject: History
meta.attribution: South African History Online - Classroom. Prepared as an open educational resource.

slides.heading: The Group Areas Act
slides.lead: How the apartheid state used a single **1950 law** to divide South Africa's cities by race.
slides.notes:
Open by asking learners what they already know about where people were allowed to live under apartheid.

Flag the two big ideas: segregation was made legal, and it had a deeply human cost.

slides.heading: Apartheid was built law by law

columns.text: After the National Party won the 1948 election, it turned racial segregation into a formal legal system called **apartheid**.

items: The **Population Registration Act (1950)** classified every person into a racial group.
The **Group Areas Act (1950)** decided *where* each group could live and trade.

columns.heading: Key term
columns.text: **Segregation** keeps racial groups physically apart, backed by the power of the state.

slides.notes: Stress the law-by-law idea: no single act created apartheid; it was an interlocking system.

slides.heading: What the Group Areas Act actually did

blocks.heading: 1. Divided the map
blocks.text: It let the government reserve any urban area for a single racial group.

blocks.heading: 2. Controlled ownership
blocks.text: People could only own property in the area assigned to their group.

blocks.heading: 3. Forced removals
blocks.text: Anyone in the wrong area could be ordered to move out.

blocks.heading: 4. Punished non-compliance
blocks.text: Staying put became a criminal offence.

slides.notes: Walk the four cards in order - they escalate from drawing lines to criminalising your own home.

slides.heading: From statute to bulldozer

blocks.caption: The law stayed on the books for over 40 years.

slides.notes: Point out the gap between 1950 (the law) and 1968-82 (mass removals).

slides.heading: What removal felt like

blocks.text: They took our houses, our streets, our neighbours - a whole world that could never be put back together.

slides.notes: This quote is a teaching composite - be explicit with learners.

slides.heading: Summary and source-based questions

items: The Group Areas Act (1950) legalised racial zoning of cities.
It powered forced removals like District Six.
Its effects still shape South Africa today.

columns.label: Q1.
columns.question: Explain how the Population Registration Act and the Group Areas Act depended on each other.

columns.label: Q2.
columns.question: Describe two ways forced removal harmed a community.

slides.notes: Use Q1 to check the law-by-law concept and Q2 to check the case study.
